lineage: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.

The Arians and the Supporters of the 'Homoousion' hold Nocturnal Assemblies and sing Antiphonal Hymns, a Species of Composition ascribed to Ignatius, surnamed Theophorus. Conflict between the Two Parties.

ccel.title: NPNF2-02. Socrates and Sozomenus Ecclesiastical Histories

source.title: NPNF2-02. Socrates and Sozomenus Ecclesiastical Histories

title: The Arians and the Supporters of the 'Homoousion' hold Nocturnal Assemblies and sing Antiphonal Hymns, a Species of Composition ascribed to Ignatius, surnamed Theophorus. Conflict between the Two Parties.

source.title: NPNF2-02. Socrates and Sozomenus Ecclesiastical Histories

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: The Arians, as we have said, held their meetings without the city.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: As often therefore as the festal days occurred—I mean Saturday and Lord’s day—in each week, on which assemblies are usually held in the churches, they congregated within the city gates about the public squares, and sang responsive verses adapted to the Arian heresy.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: This they did during the greater part of the night: and again in the morning, chanting the same songs which they called responsive, they paraded through the midst of the city, and so passed out of the gates to go to their places of assembly.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: But since they did not desist from making use of insulting expressions in relation to the Homoousians, often singing such words as these: ‘Where are they that say three things are but one power?’—John fearing lest any of the more simple should be drawn away from the church by such kind of hymns, opposed to them some of his own people, that they also employing themselves in chanting nocturnal hymns, might obscure the effort of the Arians, and confirm his own party in the profession of their faith.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: John’s design indeed seemed to be good, but it issued in tumult and dangers.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: For as the Homoousians performed their nocturnal hymns with greater display,—for there were invented by John silver crosses for them on which lighted wax-tapers were carried, provided at the expense of the empress Eudoxia,—the Arians who were very numerous, and fired with envy, resolved to revenge themselves by a desperate and riotous attack upon their rivals.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: For from the remembrance of their own recent domination, they were full of confidence in their ability to overcome, and of contempt for their adversaries.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Without delay therefore, on one of these nights, they engaged in a conflict; and Briso, one of the eunuchs of the empress, who was at that time leading the chanters of these hymns, was wounded by a stone in the forehead, and also some of the people on both sides were killed.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Whereupon the emperor being angered, forbade the Arians to chant their hymns any more in public.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Such were the events of this occasion.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: We must now however make some allusion to the origin of this custom in the church of responsive singing.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Ignatius third bishop of Antioch in Syria from the apostle Peter, who also had held intercourse with the apostles themselves, saw a vision of angels hymning in alternate chants the Holy Trinity.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Accordingly he introduced the mode of singing he had observed in the vision into the Antiochian church; whence it was transmitted by tradition to all the other churches.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Such is the account [we have received] in relation to these responsive hymns.
